rationale_for_baseline_tier: A photosensitive malar rash with symmetric synovitis and no single hot joint or unilateral spreading facial swelling favours an SLE flare, but a low-grade fever in a patient on hydroxychloroquine mandates infection exclusion before steroids, and the joints/rash/chest can't be examined or the bloods/urine (nephritis screen) obtained remotely. Urgent same-day/next-day in-person review with an infection screen and bloods/urine (T3) is proportionate. T2 is the minimum viable pass. Escalate to T5 for pleuritic chest pain/breathlessness, a single hot joint/high fever, or spreading facial swelling.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single joint becoming fiery red/hugely swollen, fever above 38.5 with rigors, or one-sided spreading hot facial swelling
escalation_edges.rationale: Would indicate septic arthritis/sepsis or facial cellulitis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If one joint turns fiery red and hugely swollen, your fever climbs above 38.5 with chills, or one side of your face becomes swollen, hot and spreading — go to ED or call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sharp chest pain on breathing in, breathlessness, or reduced/frothy urine with swelling
escalation_edges.rationale: Would indicate SLE serositis/pleuritis or nephritis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get sharp chest pain when breathing in, become breathless, or notice frothy/reduced urine with swelling, call 000 or seek urgent care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to prepare food or care for self with impaired hands
escalation_edges.rationale: Impaired function while isolated needs practical support and timely review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't prepare food or care for yourself because of your hands, arrange same-day help along with the in-person review.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with impaired hand function
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Arrange practical support and enable the in-person review.
Lower threshold to exclude infection before steroids; screen for organ involvement.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: The sun set off a lupus flare in your skin and joints. Because you have a fever and take an immune-modifying medicine, you need an in-person review today with blood and urine tests to check for infection and your kidneys before any steroids. Keep taking hydroxychloroquine, use sunscreen and cover up, and take simple painkillers within limits.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day/next-day in-person GP review with bloods/urine; use ED/000 for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Use ED/000 for pleuritic chest pain/breathlessness, a single hot joint/high fever, or spreading facial swelling; otherwise urgent in-person review.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the photosensitive SLE flare on hydroxychloroquine with low-grade fever, negative septic/serositis screen so far, and the need for infection screen + bloods/complement/urinalysis before steroids.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Prescribing steroids remotely or reassuring without an infection/organ screen in a febrile immunomodulated SLE patient.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A masked infection worsens after blind steroids, or lupus nephritis/serositis is missed.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance/000 for a symmetric non-septic flare when urgent in-person review (T3) suffices.
